content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

## Comprehensive Voice/Wake-Word Architecture Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the GAIA monorepo, here's the complete picture of how voice and wake-word functionality works today:

---

### 1. **WAKE-WORD DETECTION ARCHITECTURE**

#### Library: `libs/wake-word` (TypeScript)
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/libs/wake-word/`

**Technology Stack:**
- **Engine:** openWakeWord-compatible ONNX-based 3-stage pipeline
- **Inference Runtime:** 
  - Web: `onnxruntime-web` (WASM-SIMD)
  - Mobile: `onnxruntime-react-native`
  - Node.js: `onnxruntime-node` (tests/CLI only)
- **VAD:** Silero VAD v3/v4 (optional pre-gate to reduce false positives)

**Core Files:**
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/detector.ts` — Main `WakeWordDetector` class that buffers audio into frames and coordinates the pipeline
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/pipeline.ts` — 3-stage inference pipeline:
  1. **Melspectrogram** (ONNX) — converts PCM to 32-dim mel frames
  2. **Embedding** (ONNX) — extracts 96-dim frozen speech embeddings from mel windows
  3. **Classifier** (ONNX) — custom 122 KB Conv1D classifier head trained on "Hey GAIA"
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/vad-gate.ts` — Silero VAD gate that pre-filters silence (512-sample frames)
- `/libs/wake-word/src/types/index.ts` — Public types (constants: `SAMPLE_RATE=16kHz`, `FRAME_SAMPLES=1280`)

**Key Constants:**
```typescript
SAMPLE_RATE = 16_000
FRAME_SAMPLES = 1280  // 80 ms @ 16 kHz
MEL_FRAMES_PER_CHUNK = 76
EMBEDDING_DIM = 96
CLASSIFIER_WINDOW = 16  // sequence length for classifier
```

**Default Options:**
- Threshold: 0.6 (well-calibrated; lower = more false positives)
- Consecutive hits required: 2 (≈160 ms debounce)
- Cooldown: 1500 ms (prevents stutter-fires during conversation)
- VAD gate: enabled by default
- VAD threshold: 0.5 (speech probability above which to run classifier)

#### Platform-Specific Controllers:

**Web Controller:** `/libs/wake-word/src/web/controller.ts`
- Manages `AudioContext`, `AudioWorklet`, and `getUserMedia`
- Loads models from `/wake-word/*.onnx` (served from `apps/web/public/`)
- AudioWorklet processor: `/libs/wake-word/src/web/worklet.ts` — captures audio, downsamples to 16 kHz via linear interpolation, batches into 1280-sample frames, posts to main thread
- React hook: `/libs/wake-word/src/web/react.ts` → `useWakeWord()`

**Native Controller:** `/libs/wake-word/src/native/controller.ts`
- Uses `react-native-live-audio-stream` for mic capture
- Converts base64 16-bit PCM to Float32Array
- React hook: `/libs/wake-word/src/native/hook.ts` → `useWakeWordNative()`
- Requires iOS `NSMicrophoneUsageDescription` + `UIBackgroundModes` audio entitlement
- Requires Android `RECORD_AUDIO` + (API 34+) foreground service type `microphone`

**Node Runtime:** `/libs/wake-word/src/node/index.ts` (tests/CLI only)

---

### 2. **WEB APP INTEGRATION**

#### Wake-Word Feature in Web
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/web/src/features/wake-word/`

**Files:**
- `/features/wake-word/hooks/useHeyGaia.ts` — wraps `useWakeWord()` with app-specific model bundle
- `/features/wake-word/constants/models.ts` — points to ONNX files in `public/wake-word/`:
  ```typescript
  melspectrogram: "/wake-word/melspectrogram.onnx"
  embedding: "/wake-word/embedding_model.onnx"
  classifier: "/wake-word/hey_gaia.onnx"
  vad: "/wake-word/silero_vad.onnx"
  ```
- `/features/wake-word/components/WakeWordIndicator.tsx` — small floating indicator showing listener state (idle/listening/detecting/cooldown)

**Model Bundle:**
- ~4 MB total (ONNX files cached by browser)
- Classifier head: 122 KB Conv1D
- Trained on 18.6k positives + 25k negatives
- Positive recall: 97.7% (held-out test set)
- False positive rate: 0.0% on LibriSpeech

#### Voice Agent UI Components
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/`

**Files:**
- `/voice-agent/session-view.tsx` — main LiveKit room view, handles agent state (listening/thinking/speaking), displays media tiles or chat transcript
- `/voice-agent/agent-control-bar.tsx` — mic toggle, chat toggle, disconnect button
- `/voice-agent/media-tiles.tsx` — agent orb (when chat closed) or bar visualizer (when chat open), shows audio spectrum analysis
- `/voice-agent/agent-tile.tsx` — animated orb showing agent state with audio waveform visualization
- `/voice-agent/track-toggle.tsx` — mic on/off toggle with visual feedback
- `/components/ui/elevenlabs-ui/bar-visualizer.tsx` — audio spectrum bar visualization (24 bars, animated)

**Hooks:**
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useConnectionDetails.ts` — fetches `POST /token` endpoint for LiveKit room credentials (serverUrl, roomName, participantToken, agentToken)
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useChatAndTranscription.ts` — subscribes to LiveKit text streams for transcriptions
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useAudioVolume.ts` — analyzes mic/agent audio levels for visualization
- `/voice-agent/hooks/use-agent-control-bar.ts` — manages mic/camera toggles and device selection
- `/voice-agent/hooks/use-publish-permissions.ts` — checks LiveKit track publish permissions

#### Voice Session Entry Point
**Location:** `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/composer/VoiceModeOverlay.tsx`

- Creates a LiveKit `Room` instance
- Fetches connection details via `useConnectionDetails(conversationId)`
- Connects to LiveKit server with participant token
- Renders `SessionView` with agent UI
- Passes `agentToken` + `conversationId` in participant metadata (received by voice-agent worker)

#### API Endpoint for Token Generation
The web app calls `GET /token?conversationId=...` (backend URL in env vars) which returns:
```typescript
{
  serverUrl: string         // LiveKit server WebSocket URL
  roomName: string
  participantToken: string  // JWT for LiveKit auth
  participantIdentity: string
  participantName: string
  agentToken: string        // For backend API auth (passed to voice-agent via metadata)
}
```

---

### 3. **VOICE AGENT BACKEND** 

**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/voice-agent/` (Python)

**Technology Stack:**
- **LiveKit Agents SDK** (`livekit-agents~=1.2`)
- **STT:** Deepgram (`livekit-plugins-deepgram`, nova-3 multilingual model)
- **TTS:** ElevenLabs (`livekit-plugins-elevenlabs`, configurable voice & model)
- **VAD:** Silero VAD (loaded at startup in `prewarm()`)
- **Turn Detection:** LiveKit's `MultilingualModel` 
- **Noise Cancellation:** LiveKit's BVC (broadcast voice cancellation)
- **LLM:** Custom streaming adapter via HTTP SSE to GAIA backend

#### Key Files:

**`/src/worker.py`** — Core agent logic
```python
class CustomLLM(LLM):
    """Streams SSE from POST /api/v1/chat-stream on GAIA backend"""
    - Extracts agent token & conversation ID from participant metadata JSON
    - Sends user message to backend
    - Buffers TTS text (flush at sentence boundary ≥40 chars, hard flush at 120 chars)
    - Parses conversation_id and conversation_description from SSE payloads
    - Broadcasts conversation metadata back to room participants via text topics

def prewarm(proc):
    """Pre-loads Silero VAD model to reduce first-turn latency"""
    
async def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext):
    """Main agent entry point per LiveKit room"""
    - Loads settings from Infisical + .env
    - Creates AgentSession with:
      - deepgram.STT(model="nova-3", language="multi")
      - elevenlabs.TTS(api_key, voice_id, model)
      - MultilingualModel() turn detection
      - Silero VAD from prewarm
      - preemptive_generation=True (low-latency TTS streaming)
      - use_tts_aligned_transcript=True
    - Connects to LiveKit room
    - Extracts agentToken + conversationId from participant metadata
    - Sets up event handlers for false interruptions, metrics collection
```

**`/src/config.py`** — Settings via Pydantic + Infisical injection
```python
class VoiceAgentSettings:
    GAIA_BACKEND_URL: str           # e.g. http://localhost:8000
    ELEVENLABS_API_KEY: str
    ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID: str
    ELEVENLABS_TTS_MODEL: str       # default "eleven_turbo_v2_5"
    DEEPGRAM_API_KEY: str           # consumed directly by livekit-plugins-deepgram
```

**`/src/__main__.py`** — CLI entrypoint
```bash
python -m src start              # Start the worker
python -m src download-files     # Download VAD + turn-detection models
```

#### Dependencies:
```
livekit>=1.0.12
livekit-agents[turn-detector,silero,deepgram]~=1.2
livekit-plugins-noise-cancellation~=0.2
livekit-plugins-elevenlabs~=1.2
gaia-shared (from libs/shared/py)
```

#### LiveKit Agent Session Features:
- **Preemptive generation:** TTS starts before user finishes speaking (lower latency)
- **TTS-aligned transcript:** Uses ElevenLabs chunk boundaries for accurate transcript alignment
- **Noise cancellation:** BVC plugin active during room session
- **Metrics collection:** Tracks token usage, latency, and costs

---

### 4. **LIVEKIT INTEGRATION ACROSS PLATFORMS**

**Web Dependencies:**
- `livekit-client@^2.15.4`
- `@livekit/components-react@^2.9.14`
- `onnxruntime-web@^1.20.0`

**Mobile Dependencies:**
- Same LiveKit SDKs + `react-native-live-audio-stream` for mic capture

**Key LiveKit Components Used:**
- `RoomContext` — provides LiveKit Room instance to children
- `RoomAudioRenderer` — plays agent's audio output
- `useVoiceAssistant()` — hook from `@livekit/components-react` that provides agent state (connecting/listening/thinking/speaking) and audio track
- `useRemoteParticipants()` — gets list of remote participants (agent + any other users)
- `Room.registerTextStreamHandler()` — subscribes to named text topics (conversation-id, conversation-description)

**Room Connection Flow:**
1. Frontend calls `GET /token` to get LiveKit credentials + agent token
2. Frontend creates `Room` and calls `room.connect(serverUrl, participantToken)`
3. Voice-agent worker detects new participant, extracts agentToken + conversationId from metadata
4. Worker initializes CustomLLM with credentials, starts listening
5. Agent speaks to room; frontend renders media tiles/visualizer
6. Session ends → conversation stored in IndexedDB with conversation-id

---

### 5. **VOICE INPUT FLOW (END-TO-END)**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        AUDIO CAPTURE                             │
│  Microphone → AudioContext (web) or LiveAudioStream (mobile)     │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    DOWNSAMPLING (16 kHz mono)                    │
│     AudioWorklet (web) or native module (mobile)                 │
│     Linear interpolation to 16 kHz, batch into 1280 samples      │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WAKE-WORD DETECTION (Optional, Frontend)            │
│  WakeWordDetector.push() → runs pipeline every 80 ms             │
│  1. Melspectrogram ONNX                                          │
│  2. Embedding ONNX (with Silero VAD pre-gate)                    │
│  3. Classifier (122 KB ONNX)                                     │
│  → Score in [0, 1]; fire if above threshold + 2 consecutive hits │
│  → ~1.6 ms inference per frame (Node CPU)                        │
│  → ~2 s warmup after start                                       │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼ (if wake-word fired or user initiates session)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 LIVEKIT ROOM SETUP & STREAMING                   │
│  Frontend connects to LiveKit room with participant token        │
│  Sends mic audio track to room                                   │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               SPEECH-TO-TEXT (Backend: Deepgram)                 │
│  Voice-agent receives audio from LiveKit                         │
│  Deepgram STT (nova-3, multilingual) transcribes in real-time    │
│  LiveKit turn_detector.MultilingualModel() detects speech end    │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             LLM GENERATION (Backend → GAIA API)                  │
│  CustomLLM streams SSE from POST /api/v1/chat-stream             │
│  Passes transcribed text to backend                              │
│  Streams response back in small chunks (30-120 char batches)     │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          TEXT-TO-SPEECH (Backend: ElevenLabs Turbo)              │
│  CustomLLM sends buffered text chunks to ElevenLabs TTS          │
│  ElevenLabs returns audio stream                                 │
│  Voice-agent publishes audio track to LiveKit room               │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 AUDIO PLAYBACK & VISUALIZATION                   │
│  Frontend receives agent's audio track from LiveKit              │
│  RoomAudioRenderer plays it                                      │
│  BarVisualizer or AgentTile shows audio spectrum (demo or real)  │
│  Transcriptions appear in ChatRenderer or as separate bubbles    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

### 6. **EXISTING VOICE UI COMPONENTS**

#### Desktop/Web Components
- **WakeWordIndicator** — floating badge showing listener state (listening 🎤, detecting ⏳, off 🔇)
- **AgentTile (Orb)** — animated sphere with gradient, pulses with mic audio, shows detecting state
- **BarVisualizer** — 24-bar audio spectrum analyzer, animates in real-time
- **AgentControlBar** — mic toggle, chat toggle, hang-up button (fixed bottom)
- **SessionView** — main container with media tiles above, control bar below, chat transcript optional
- **VoiceGradient** — background gradient that animates based on audio spectrum (dev demo)

#### Mobile Components
- Same architecture as web via Expo React Native

---

### 7. **MISSING / NOT IMPLEMENTED**

**Desktop Electron App:**
- No wake-word or voice-agent integration in `/apps/desktop/src/` today
- Bundles the Next.js build as-is (uses web's static exports)
- Could leverage `@gaia/wake-word/native` + LiveKit if added

**Always-On Wake-Word Bubble (Your Use Case):**
- Wake-word detection exists but is not wired to a persistent floating indicator
- `WakeWordIndicator` component exists but not placed in a main layout
- No persistent background listening during non-voice-session states
- No global keyboard shortcut or gesture to trigger voice mode
- Voice sessions are initiated only via button in chat UI, not by wake-word

---

### 8. **FILE SUMMARY (Concrete Paths)**

| Component | Path | Type |
|-----------|------|------|
| Core wake-word library | `/libs/wake-word/src/` | TypeScript |
| Web detector | `/libs/wake-word/src/web/controller.ts` | TS |
| Web AudioWorklet | `/libs/wake-word/src/web/worklet.ts` | TS (worklet) |
| Web React hook | `/libs/wake-word/src/web/react.ts` | TS |
| Native (mobile) controller | `/libs/wake-word/src/native/controller.ts` | TS |
| Native React hook | `/libs/wake-word/src/native/hook.ts` | TS |
| Core detector | `/libs/wake-word/src/core/detector.ts` | TS |
| 3-stage pipeline | `/libs/wake-word/src/core/pipeline.ts` | TS |
| VAD gate (Silero) | `/libs/wake-word/src/core/vad-gate.ts` | TS |
| Voice-agent worker | `/apps/voice-agent/src/worker.py` | Python |
| Voice-agent config | `/apps/voice-agent/src/config.py` | Python |
| Web wake-word hook | `/apps/web/src/features/wake-word/hooks/useHeyGaia.ts` | TS |
| Web model bundle | `/apps/web/src/features/wake-word/constants/models.ts` | TS |
| Wake-word indicator | `/apps/web/src/features/wake-word/components/WakeWordIndicator.tsx` | TSX |
| Voice session view | `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/session-view.tsx` | TSX |
| Control bar | `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/agent-control-bar.tsx` | TSX |
| Media tiles (orb/bar) | `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/media-tiles.tsx` | TSX |
| Connection details hook | `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/hooks/useConnectionDetails.ts` | TS |
| Voice mode overlay | `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/composer/VoiceModeOverlay.tsx` | TSX |
| ONNX models | `/apps/web/public/wake-word/*.onnx` | Binary assets |
| Live audio stream (mobile) | `/apps/mobile/src/features/wake-word/hooks/useHeyGaia.ts` | TS |
| Mobile model bundle | `/apps/mobile/src/features/wake-word/constants/models.ts` | TS |

---

### 9. **KEY INSIGHTS FOR YOUR ALWAYS-ON DESKTOP BUBBLE**

To build an always-on wake-word desktop voice bubble triggered by "Hey GAIA", you would:

1. **Use `@gaia/wake-word/web`** — it's already isomorphic and works in Electron
2. **Persistent Layout** — add `WakeWordIndicator` to the main app shell (always visible)
3. **Listen for Detection** — wire `useHeyGaia({ enabled: true })` at app root, auto-trigger voice session on detection
4. **Session Modal** — overlay the `VoiceApp` component when detected
5. **Backend Token Endpoint** — ensure voice-agent can access `GET /token` from desktop (if isolated)
6. **Preload Models** — cache 4 MB of ONNX files on first run (browser caches subsequent)

The infrastructure is ready — just needs UI plumbing and state management to glue it together.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

## Comprehensive Voice/Wake-Word Architecture Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the GAIA monorepo, here's the complete picture of how voice and wake-word functionality works today:

---

### 1. **WAKE-WORD DETECTION ARCHITECTURE**

#### Library: `libs/wake-word` (TypeScript)
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/libs/wake-word/`

**Technology Stack:**
- **Engine:** openWakeWord-compatible ONNX-based 3-stage pipeline
- **Inference Runtime:** 
  - Web: `onnxruntime-web` (WASM-SIMD)
  - Mobile: `onnxruntime-react-native`
  - Node.js: `onnxruntime-node` (tests/CLI only)
- **VAD:** Silero VAD v3/v4 (optional pre-gate to reduce false positives)

**Core Files:**
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/detector.ts` — Main `WakeWordDetector` class that buffers audio into frames and coordinates the pipeline
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/pipeline.ts` — 3-stage inference pipeline:
  1. **Melspectrogram** (ONNX) — converts PCM to 32-dim mel frames
  2. **Embedding** (ONNX) — extracts 96-dim frozen speech embeddings from mel windows
  3. **Classifier** (ONNX) — custom 122 KB Conv1D classifier head trained on "Hey GAIA"
- `/libs/wake-word/src/core/vad-gate.ts` — Silero VAD gate that pre-filters silence (512-sample frames)
- `/libs/wake-word/src/types/index.ts` — Public types (constants: `SAMPLE_RATE=16kHz`, `FRAME_SAMPLES=1280`)

**Key Constants:**
```typescript
SAMPLE_RATE = 16_000
FRAME_SAMPLES = 1280  // 80 ms @ 16 kHz
MEL_FRAMES_PER_CHUNK = 76
EMBEDDING_DIM = 96
CLASSIFIER_WINDOW = 16  // sequence length for classifier
```

**Default Options:**
- Threshold: 0.6 (well-calibrated; lower = more false positives)
- Consecutive hits required: 2 (≈160 ms debounce)
- Cooldown: 1500 ms (prevents stutter-fires during conversation)
- VAD gate: enabled by default
- VAD threshold: 0.5 (speech probability above which to run classifier)

#### Platform-Specific Controllers:

**Web Controller:** `/libs/wake-word/src/web/controller.ts`
- Manages `AudioContext`, `AudioWorklet`, and `getUserMedia`
- Loads models from `/wake-word/*.onnx` (served from `apps/web/public/`)
- AudioWorklet processor: `/libs/wake-word/src/web/worklet.ts` — captures audio, downsamples to 16 kHz via linear interpolation, batches into 1280-sample frames, posts to main thread
- React hook: `/libs/wake-word/src/web/react.ts` → `useWakeWord()`

**Native Controller:** `/libs/wake-word/src/native/controller.ts`
- Uses `react-native-live-audio-stream` for mic capture
- Converts base64 16-bit PCM to Float32Array
- React hook: `/libs/wake-word/src/native/hook.ts` → `useWakeWordNative()`
- Requires iOS `NSMicrophoneUsageDescription` + `UIBackgroundModes` audio entitlement
- Requires Android `RECORD_AUDIO` + (API 34+) foreground service type `microphone`

**Node Runtime:** `/libs/wake-word/src/node/index.ts` (tests/CLI only)

---

### 2. **WEB APP INTEGRATION**

#### Wake-Word Feature in Web
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/web/src/features/wake-word/`

**Files:**
- `/features/wake-word/hooks/useHeyGaia.ts` — wraps `useWakeWord()` with app-specific model bundle
- `/features/wake-word/constants/models.ts` — points to ONNX files in `public/wake-word/`:
  ```typescript
  melspectrogram: "/wake-word/melspectrogram.onnx"
  embedding: "/wake-word/embedding_model.onnx"
  classifier: "/wake-word/hey_gaia.onnx"
  vad: "/wake-word/silero_vad.onnx"
  ```
- `/features/wake-word/components/WakeWordIndicator.tsx` — small floating indicator showing listener state (idle/listening/detecting/cooldown)

**Model Bundle:**
- ~4 MB total (ONNX files cached by browser)
- Classifier head: 122 KB Conv1D
- Trained on 18.6k positives + 25k negatives
- Positive recall: 97.7% (held-out test set)
- False positive rate: 0.0% on LibriSpeech

#### Voice Agent UI Components
**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/voice-agent/`

**Files:**
- `/voice-agent/session-view.tsx` — main LiveKit room view, handles agent state (listening/thinking/speaking), displays media tiles or chat transcript
- `/voice-agent/agent-control-bar.tsx` — mic toggle, chat toggle, disconnect button
- `/voice-agent/media-tiles.tsx` — agent orb (when chat closed) or bar visualizer (when chat open), shows audio spectrum analysis
- `/voice-agent/agent-tile.tsx` — animated orb showing agent state with audio waveform visualization
- `/voice-agent/track-toggle.tsx` — mic on/off toggle with visual feedback
- `/components/ui/elevenlabs-ui/bar-visualizer.tsx` — audio spectrum bar visualization (24 bars, animated)

**Hooks:**
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useConnectionDetails.ts` — fetches `POST /token` endpoint for LiveKit room credentials (serverUrl, roomName, participantToken, agentToken)
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useChatAndTranscription.ts` — subscribes to LiveKit text streams for transcriptions
- `/voice-agent/hooks/useAudioVolume.ts` — analyzes mic/agent audio levels for visualization
- `/voice-agent/hooks/use-agent-control-bar.ts` — manages mic/camera toggles and device selection
- `/voice-agent/hooks/use-publish-permissions.ts` — checks LiveKit track publish permissions

#### Voice Session Entry Point
**Location:** `/apps/web/src/features/chat/components/composer/VoiceModeOverlay.tsx`

- Creates a LiveKit `Room` instance
- Fetches connection details via `useConnectionDetails(conversationId)`
- Connects to LiveKit server with participant token
- Renders `SessionView` with agent UI
- Passes `agentToken` + `conversationId` in participant metadata (received by voice-agent worker)

#### API Endpoint for Token Generation
The web app calls `GET /token?conversationId=...` (backend URL in env vars) which returns:
```typescript
{
  serverUrl: string         // LiveKit server WebSocket URL
  roomName: string
  participantToken: string  // JWT for LiveKit auth
  participantIdentity: string
  participantName: string
  agentToken: string        // For backend API auth (passed to voice-agent via metadata)
}
```

---

### 3. **VOICE AGENT BACKEND** 

**Location:** `/Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia/apps/voice-agent/` (Python)

**Technology Stack:**
- **LiveKit Agents SDK** (`livekit-agents~=1.2`)
- **STT:** Deepgram (`livekit-plugins-deepgram`, nova-3 multilingual model)
- **TTS:** ElevenLabs (`livekit-plugins-elevenlabs`, configurable voice & model)
- **VAD:** Silero VAD (loaded at startup in `prewarm()`)
- **Turn Detection:** LiveKit's `MultilingualModel` 
- **Noise Cancellation:** LiveKit's BVC (broadcast voice cancellation)
- **LLM:** Custom streaming adapter via HTTP SSE to GAIA backend

#### Key Files:

**`/src/worker.py`** — Core agent logic
```python
class CustomLLM(LLM):
    """Streams SSE from POST /api/v1/chat-stream on GAIA backend"""
    - Extracts agent token & conversation ID from participant metadata JSON
    - Sends user message to backend
    - Buffers TTS text (flush at sentence boundary ≥40 chars, hard flush at 120 chars)
    - Parses conversation_id and conversation_description from SSE payloads
    - Broadcasts conversation metadata back to room participants via text topics

def prewarm(proc):
    """Pre-loads Silero VAD model to reduce first-turn latency"""
    
async def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext):
    """Main agent entry point per LiveKit room"""
    - Loads settings from Infisical + .env
    - Creates AgentSession with:
      - deepgram.STT(model="nova-3", language="multi")
      - elevenlabs.TTS(api_key, voice_id, model)
      - MultilingualModel() turn detection
      - Silero VAD from prewarm
      - preemptive_generation=True (low-latency TTS streaming)
      - use_tts_aligned_transcript=True
    - Connects to LiveKit room
    - Extracts agentToken + conversationId from participant metadata
    - Sets up event handlers for false interruptions, metrics collection
```

**`/src/config.py`** — Settings via Pydantic + Infisical injection
```python
class VoiceAgentSettings:
    GAIA_BACKEND_URL: str           # e.g. http://localhost:8000
    ELEVENLABS_API_KEY: str
    ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID: str
    ELEVENLABS_TTS_MODEL: str       # default "eleven_turbo_v2_5"
    DEEPGRAM_API_KEY: str           # consumed directly by livekit-plugins-deepgram
```

**`/src/__main__.py`** — CLI entrypoint
```bash
python -m src start              # Start the worker
python -m src download-files     # Download VAD + turn-detection models
```

#### Dependencies:
```
livekit>=1.0.12
livekit-agents[turn-detector,silero,deepgram]~=1.2
livekit-plugins-noise-cancellation~=0.2
livekit-plugins-elevenlabs~=1.2
gaia-shared (from libs/shared/py)
```

#### LiveKit Agent Session Features:
- **Preemptive generation:** TTS starts before user finishes speaking (lower latency)
- **TTS-aligned transcript:** Uses ElevenLabs chunk boundaries for accurate transcript alignment
- **Noise cancellation:** BVC plugin active during room session
- **Metrics collection:** Tracks token usage, latency, and costs

---

### 4. **LIVEKIT INTEGRATION ACROSS PLATFORMS**

**Web Dependencies:**
- `livekit-client@^2.15.4`
- `@livekit/components-react@^2.9.14`
- `onnxruntime-web@^1.20.0`

**Mobile Dependencies:**
- Same LiveKit SDKs + `react-native-live-audio-stream` for mic capture

**Key LiveKit Components Used:**
- `RoomContext` — provides LiveKit Room instance to children
- `RoomAudioRenderer` — plays agent's audio output
- `useVoiceAssistant()` — hook from `@livekit/components-react` that provides agent state (connecting/listening/thinking/speaking) and audio track
- `useRemoteParticipants()` — gets list of remote participants (agent + any other users)
- `Room.registerTextStreamHandler()` — subscribes to named text topics (conversation-id, conversation-description)

**Room Connection Flow:**
1. Frontend calls `GET /token` to get LiveKit credentials + agent token
2. Frontend creates `Room` and calls `room.connect(serverUrl, participantToken)`
3. Voice-agent worker detects new participant, extracts agentToken + conversationId from metadata
4. Worker initializes CustomLLM with credentials, starts listening
5. Agent speaks to room; frontend renders media tiles/visualizer
6. Session ends → conversation stored in IndexedDB with conversation-id

---

### 5. **VOICE INPUT FLOW (END-TO-END)**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        AUDIO CAPTURE                             │
│  Microphone → AudioContext (web) or LiveAudioStream (mobile)     │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    DOWNSAMPLING (16 kHz mono)                    │
│     AudioWorklet (web) or native module (mobile)                 │
│     Linear interpolation to 16 kHz, batch into 1280 samples      │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WAKE-WORD DETECTION (Optional, Frontend)            │
│  WakeWordDetector.push() → runs pipeline every 80 ms             │
│  1. Melspectrogram ONNX                                          │
│  2. Embedding ONNX (with Silero VAD pre-gate)                    │
│  3. Classifier (122 KB ONNX)                                     │
│  → Score in [0, 1]; fire if above threshold + 2 consecutive hits │
│  → ~1.6 ms inference per frame (Node CPU)                        │
│  → ~2 s warmup after start                                       │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼ (if wake-word fired or user initiates session)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 LIVEKIT ROOM SETUP & STREAMING                   │
│  Frontend connects to LiveKit room with participant token        │
│  Sends mic audio track to room                                   │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               SPEECH-TO-TEXT (Backend: Deepgram)                 │
│  Voice-agent receives audio from LiveKit                         │
│  Deepgram STT (nova-3, multilingual) transcribes in real-time    │
│  LiveKit turn_detector.MultilingualModel() detects speech end    │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             LLM GENERATION (Backend → GAIA API)                  │
│  CustomLLM streams SSE from POST /api/v1/chat-stream             │
│  Passes transcribed text to backend                              │
│  Streams response back in small chunks (30-120 char batches)     │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          TEXT-TO-SPEECH (Backend: ElevenLabs Turbo)              │
│  CustomLLM sends buffered text chunks to ElevenLabs TTS          │
│  ElevenLabs returns audio stream                                 │
│  Voice-agent publishes audio track to LiveKit room               │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 AUDIO PLAYBACK & VISUALIZATION                   │
│  Frontend receives agent's audio track from LiveKit              │
│  RoomAudioRenderer plays it                                      │
│  BarVisualizer or AgentTile shows audio spectrum (demo or real)  │
│  Transcriptions appear in ChatRenderer or as separate bubbles    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

### 6. **EXISTING VOICE UI COMPONENTS**

#### Desktop/Web Components
- **WakeWordIndicator** — floating badge showing listener state (listening 🎤, detecting ⏳, off 🔇)
- **AgentTile (Orb)** — animated sphere with gradient, pulses with mic audio, shows detecting state

- **VoiceGradient** — background gradient that animates based on audio spectrum (dev demo)

#### Mobile Components
- Same architecture as web via Expo React Native

---

### 7. **MISSING / NOT IMPLEMENTED**

**Desktop Electron App:**
- No wake-word or voice-agent integration in `/apps/desktop/src/` today
- Bundles the Next.js build as-is (uses web's static exports)
- Could leverage `@gaia/wake-word/native` + LiveKit if added

aiTitle: Add always-active Gaia assistant to desktop app with Electron

content.text:
Here's the full picture. I traced your desktop shell, the wake-word lib, the voice stack, and the agent backend — and the good news is you've already built ~70% of the hard parts. What's missing is mostly **Electron window plumbing** and one genuinely new capability (**local computer use**). Let me lay it out.

---

## Where you are today

- **Desktop** (`apps/desktop`) is a thin Electron 39 shell. One `BrowserWindow` (`src/main/windows/main.ts`) loads the Next.js standalone app via `loadURL`. All UI lives in `apps/web`. No tray, no global shortcuts, no overlay window, and it almost certainly quits on `window-all-closed`.
- **Your splash window already proves the overlay pattern** — `src/main/windows/splash.ts` runs `frame: false, transparent: true, vibrancy: "under-window"`. That's exactly the recipe for a notch/bubble.
- **Wake word is done** — `libs/wake-word` is an isomorphic openWakeWord ONNX pipeline (mel → embedding → "Hey GAIA" classifier + Silero VAD). The web controller uses `AudioContext`/`AudioWorklet`/`getUserMedia`, so **it runs in an Electron renderer as-is**. `useHeyGaia()` and a `WakeWordIndicator` component exist but aren't wired to anything persistent.
- **Voice is done** — `VoiceModeOverlay` → LiveKit room (`GET /token`) → `apps/voice-agent` (Deepgram STT, ElevenLabs TTS) → `CustomLLM` streams from `POST /api/v1/chat-stream`. Crucially, **agent-token auth already exists** (`middleware/agent_auth.py`) for headless clients.
- **The agent brain is done** — LangGraph two-agent graph (comms → executor), semantic tool retrieval over ChromaDB, Gmail/Calendar/etc. tools, SSE streaming over Redis. So "fetch my emails" is **already a tool call** — voice just needs to reach it.
- **Computer use does NOT exist.** It's advertised in `oauth_config.py` capabilities and OpenAI CUA types are sitting in the voice-agent venv, but there is no actual screen-control tool in the registry. This is the only net-new capability.

So the project isn't "build everything" — it's "restructure the desktop shell into a multi-window agent, run wake-word always-on, and add one new local-control capability."

---

## 1. The architectural shift: thin shell → multi-window agent

You move from one window to **three window roles plus a persistent background**:

| Window | Purpose | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| **Notch** | Always-visible sliver docked at top-center, hosts the always-on wake-word listener + idle state | Lives for the whole app session, survives main window closing |
| **Bubble/HUD** | Expands top-right when "Hey GAIA" fires; the voice/agent conversation surface | Shown on trigger, hidden after |
| **Main** | Your existing full app | As today |

The notch window becomes the app's **heartbeat** — it owns the mic and never dies. That means two changes to lifecycle:
- `app.on("window-all-closed")` must **not** `quit()` (keep the notch alive headless).
- Add a **tray icon** + a **global shortcut** (`globalShortcut.register`) as a manual trigger alongside the wake word.

---

## 2. The notch + bubble windows (Electron specifics)

This is the part you asked about most ("especially with Electron"), so here are the flags that actually matter.

**Notch window** (`apps/desktop/src/main/windows/notch.ts`):
```
frame: false, transparent: true, resizable: false, movable: false,
hasShadow: false, skipTaskbar: true,
focusable: false,                 // never steal keyboard focus
webPreferences: { preload, contextIsolation: true }
```
Then at runtime:
- `win.setAlwaysOnTop(true, "screen-saver")` — floats **above the menu bar and fullscreen apps**. The level string is the key; default always-on-top sits *below* the menu bar.
- `win.setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces(true, { visibleOnFullScreen: true })` — shows on every Space and over fullscreen apps. Without this it vanishes when the user goes fullscreen.
- macOS: set the window `type: "panel"` so it behaves like a HUD, not an app window (doesn't show in Mission Control / app switcher).
- **Click-through**: `win.setIgnoreMouseEvents(true, { forward: true })` so the mouse passes through the transparent regions to whatever's underneath, and toggle it off from the renderer (`onMouseEnter` of interactive regions → IPC → `setIgnoreMouseEvents(false)`). This is the standard overlay trick.

**Positioning over the notch**: you can't paint pixels *under* the camera cutout, so the technique (same as NotchNook / Boring Notch) is to render a black panel at top-center that **visually merges** with the physical notch and expands downward. Derive geometry from:
```
const d = screen.getPrimaryDisplay();
const menuBarHeight = d.bounds.height - d.workArea.height - dockHeight;
// notched Macs report ~37px menu bar vs ~24px non-notched — use that to detect/size
// center x = (d.bounds.width - notchWidth) / 2, y = 0
```

**Bubble window** (`windows/bubble.ts`): same transparent/always-on-top recipe, but `focusable: true` (the user types/talks to it), positioned top-right, animated in. **Keep your "all UI lives in web" philosophy** — the bubble just `loadURL`s a dedicated route, e.g. `serverUrl + "/overlay/bubble"`, and the notch loads `/overlay/notch`. No new UI framework in the desktop package; you add two thin Next.js routes in `apps/web/src/app/`.

---

## 3. Always-on wake word

Host the listener in the **notch renderer** (it's always alive). It's literally `useHeyGaia({ enabled: true })` from `@gaia/wake-word/web` on the `/overlay/notch` route. On detection:

```
detector fires → renderer IPC "wake:detected" → main process
  → show + animate bubble window
  → bubble route mounts the existing VoiceModeOverlay/SessionView
```

The one real gotcha is **macOS mic permission in a packaged app**. You'll need, in addition to the hardened runtime you already have:
- `com.apple.security.device.audio-input` in `resources/entitlements.mac.plist`
- `NSMicrophoneUsageDescription` via electron-builder `extendInfo` in `electron-builder.yml`
- `systemPreferences.askForMediaAccess("microphone")` + a `session.setPermissionRequestHandler` in main

---

## 4. The bubble session flow — reuse the voice stack verbatim

When the bubble opens, it does exactly what `VoiceModeOverlay` does today: `useConnectionDetails` → `GET /token` → connect LiveKit Room → `apps/voice-agent` handles STT/TTS and streams from `chat-stream`. **Nothing new on the backend for voice.** The only difference is the host: an always-on-top bubble window instead of a modal inside the main app.

This means **"fetch my emails" works on day one** — it's just a voice turn that the executor routes to the existing Gmail tool. No computer use required. Worth internalizing: most of what users will ask ("summarize my inbox", "what's on my calendar") should go through your **existing API tools**, which is faster, cheaper, and more reliable than driving a GUI. Computer use is the *fallback* for apps without an API.

---

## 5. Computer use — the one real design decision

Your phrase "help me with what's on my screen by even controlling my screen" means **local** computer use: screenshot the user's actual Mac, move their actual mouse, type on their actual keyboard. This is fundamentally different from the cloud/browser automation your `oauth_config` already advertises (Browserbase — that drives a *virtual* screen in the cloud). You likely want **both**, for different jobs:

- **Cloud/browser (Browserbase + Stagehand / browser-use)** — scraping, "log into this site and pull X", scale automation. Runs server-side, no user-machine permissions.
- **Local desktop control** — "help with what's on my screen right now", driving native apps (Mail.app, Figma, Xcode). **Must** execute on the desktop; the cloud can't touch the user's machine.

### Recommended pattern for local control: a "device gateway"

Keep **one brain**. Don't fork agent logic into the desktop. Instead:

1. The desktop opens a **persistent WebSocket** to the backend on launch and registers as a controllable *device* (authenticated with the agent token you already issue).
2. Add a `computer_use` tool to the LangGraph executor (`apps/api/app/agents/tools/computer_use_tool.py`). When invoked, it doesn't act locally — it **relays the action down the device socket** and awaits the result.
3. The desktop's executor (`apps/desktop/src/main/computer-use/`) performs the action with a native lib and sends back a fresh screenshot.
4. The backend runs the **Anthropic computer-use agentic loop** (screenshot → model picks action → relay → screenshot → repeat) right where your LLM clients already live. Screenshots flow desktop → backend → Anthropic; actions flow back.

```
Anthropic computer-use model (backend)
        ▲ screenshot          │ action {click x,y / type / scroll}
        │                     ▼
   computer_use tool  ◄──WS──►  desktop main process
   (LangGraph executor)         (nut.js: screenshot + mouse + keyboard)
```

Why this shape: the screenshot has to reach the model (Anthropic API) regardless of where the loop lives, so cloud-side orchestration costs you nothing extra in privacy, and it **reuses your entire existing stack** — memory, tool retrieval, streaming, the tool-call render contract in chat, cancellation. The cost is round-trip latency; mitigate by downscaling screenshots to the resolution Anthropic's computer-use guidance wants (~XGA / 1280-wide) — that *also* improves the model's click accuracy.

Model: use the Anthropic **`computer_20250124` tool** with a current Claude (Opus/Sonnet 4.x) via direct Anthropic API or the Vercel AI Gateway. Your current LLM client routes Claude through OpenRouter; computer use needs the first-party beta tool, so this is a dedicated client path.

### Open-source resources to lean on (don't build from scratch)

- **Anthropic `computer-use-demo`** — the canonical agentic loop + `computer`/`bash`/`text_editor` tool schemas. Copy the loop shape.
- **`Agent.exe`** — an open-source Electron app that drives macOS with Claude computer use. Almost exactly your target; great reference for the Electron→native bridge.
- **nut.js** (`@nut-tree-fork/nut-js`) — maintained cross-platform mouse/keyboard/screen control in Node, runs in the Electron main process. (RobotJS is the older, less-maintained alternative.)
- **OmniParser** (Microsoft) / **UI-TARS** (ByteDance, open-weights GUI agent) — screen→element parsing if you want to augment raw-pixel clicking with structured targets for reliability.
- **Open Interpreter (`--os` mode)**, **self-operating-computer**, **Bytebot**, **cua** — full local-control references.
- **Stagehand / browser-use / Playwright MCP** — for the browser/cloud lane.

### Screen *reading* (the cheap, high-value middle ground)

"Help me with what's on my screen" mostly needs **vision, not control**: capture one screenshot (`desktopCapturer` — no Accessibility permission, just Screen Recording), attach it as image context to a normal agent turn. This is a fraction of the complexity of full control and probably covers the majority of "what's on my screen" intents. Build this *before* the control loop.

---

## 6. macOS permissions — the real gate

Full local control needs **three** TCC permissions, each a system prompt + entitlement, and all must survive notarization (you already ship hardened runtime):

| Capability | Permission | API |
|---|---|---|
| Wake word + voice | Microphone | `askForMediaAccess("microphone")` |
| See the screen | Screen Recording | `desktopCapturer` / `screen` |
| Control mouse/keyboard | Accessibility | nut.js (user grants in System Settings) |

Accessibility can't be requested programmatically — you deep-link the user to System Settings and detect grant. Plan a real onboarding flow for this; it's the biggest UX friction in the whole feature.

---

## 7. Concrete file-level plan, mapped to your repo

**Desktop (`apps/desktop/src/main/`)**
- `windows/notch.ts`, `windows/bubble.ts` — new overlay windows (recipe above)
- `tray.ts` — tray icon + quit/restore
- `shortcuts.ts` — `globalShortcut` manual trigger
- `device-gateway.ts` — persistent WS to backend, agent-token auth
- `computer-use/executor.ts` — nut.js action dispatch (`click`/`type`/`scroll`/`key`)
- `computer-use/screenshot.ts` — `desktopCapturer` + downscale
- `permissions.ts` — mic/screen/accessibility request + status
- extend `ipc.ts` + `preload/index.ts` with `wake:detected`, `overlay:setIgnoreMouse`, `device:*` channels
- change `window-all-closed` to keep the app alive

**Web (`apps/web/src/`)**
- `app/.../overlay/notch/page.tsx` — hosts `useHeyGaia` always-on + idle UI
- `app/.../overlay/bubble/page.tsx` — reuses `VoiceModeOverlay`/`SessionView`
- `features/overlay/` — small Zustand store for overlay state, `useElectronOverlay` hook

**Backend (`apps/api/app/`)**
- `agents/tools/computer_use_tool.py` — relays actions over the device channel, registered in the ChromaDB registry
- `api/v1/endpoints/devices.py` (or WS) — device registration + action relay channel
- a dedicated Anthropic computer-use client path (first-party `computer_20250124` tool) in `agents/llm/`

---

## 8. Suggested phasing

1. **Shell + voice in the notch.** Notch window, always-on wake word, bubble that runs your *existing* LiveKit voice session. Delivers "Hey GAIA → talk → it uses my Gmail/Calendar tools." No computer use. ~80% of the wow factor, ~20% of the risk.
2. **Screen reading.** One-shot screenshot as agent vision context for "what's on my screen." Only needs Screen Recording permission.
3. **Local computer use.** Device gateway + nut.js + Anthropic computer-use loop. Accessibility permission, onboarding, the works.

---

This is a meaty enough effort that I'd want it written up properly before any code. Want me to drop a full implementation plan into `.agents/plans/` (architecture decisions, the device-gateway protocol, window flags, permission flow, phased tickets)? And one fork worth deciding now since it shapes the plan: for local control, are you committed to the **backend-orchestrated device-gateway** pattern I recommended, or do you want to evaluate running the **computer-use loop locally on the desktop** (lower screenshot latency, but agent logic split across two places)?

content.content:
# OpenSpec: A Spec-Driven Planning Framework

## Overview

OpenSpec is a lightweight, agent-agnostic framework for planning software changes through living specifications. It integrates with 25+ coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, etc.) and stores specs directly in your codebase as persistent documentation.

## Directory Structure

Based on the content shown, OpenSpec creates:

```
openspec/
├── specs/
│   ├── auth-login/
│   │   └── spec.md
│   ├── auth-session/
│   │   └── spec.md
│   └── [feature-name]/
│       └── spec.md
└── changes/
    └── [change-id]/
        ├── proposal.md
        ├── design.md
        ├── tasks.md
        └── specs/
            └── [affected-specs]/
                └── spec.md
```

The page mentions specs are "organized by capability" and "live in the repository alongside your code."

## Spec Markdown Format

Specification files use this hierarchy:

- `# [feature-name] Specification`
- `## Purpose` — describes the feature's role
- `## Requirements`
  - `### Requirement: [description]` — statements using "SHALL"
    - `#### Scenario: [name]`
      - `- GIVEN [initial state]`
      - `- WHEN [action occurs]`
      - `- THEN [expected outcome]`

**Spec deltas** show changes with `+` (additions) and `-` (removals):

```markdown
- The system SHALL expire sessions after 24 hours
+ The system SHALL support configurable expiration periods
```

## Change Proposal Structure

When proposing changes via `/openspec:proposal`, the framework generates:

1. **proposal.md** — describes the intended change
2. **design.md** — technical decisions and architecture
3. **tasks.md** — implementation tasks broken down by phase
4. **specs/** — delta files showing requirement changes

## Key Characteristics

The framework emphasizes that developers should "review intent, not just code" through spec deltas that capture "requirement changes of the system." Specs provide "context that persists" across chat sessions and team transitions, serving as "living documentation" of what code is supposed to do.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about OpenSpec and spec-driven development with proposal.md, tasks.md, and spec delta format:

## Overview

OpenSpec adds a lightweight spec layer so you agree on what to build before any code is written. The documentation summarizes the flow as: proposal → specs → design → tasks → implement.

## Key Artifacts

OpenSpec separates two spaces: openspec/specs/ as the source of truth for the system's current behavior, and openspec/changes/ as a folder for proposed changes. Each change lives as a self-contained package with proposal.md, design.md, tasks.md, and delta specs.

When you describe a change you want to make, OpenSpec generates everything needed to review it: a proposal document, broken-down implementation tasks, technical design decisions, and the spec deltas showing how requirements will change.

## Delta Spec Format

Delta specs represent proposed modifications. These interim documents mark sections as "ADDED," "MODIFIED," or "REMOVED," clearly communicating what has changed. This makes it easy for both humans and AI agents to understand exactly what's being proposed without needing to diff entire documents.

## Practical Example

specs/ui/spec.md - Delta showing new requirements: # Delta for UI ## ADDED Requirements ### Requirement: Theme Selection The system SHALL allow users to choose between light and dark themes.

Spec deltas use EARS format (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax). For complete EARS guidelines, Spec deltas use EARS format with entries like ## ADDED Requirements ### Requirement: User Login WHEN a user submits valid credentials, the system SHALL authenticate the user and create a session. #### Scenario: Successful Login GIVEN a user with email "user@example.com" and password "correct123" WHEN the user submits the login form THEN the system creates an authenticated session AND redirects to the dashboard.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# OpenSpec Delta Spec Format

## Delta Section Headers

The three required section headers in delta specs are:

- `## ADDED Requirements`
- `## MODIFIED Requirements`
- `## REMOVED Requirements`

## Requirement and Scenario Format

Each requirement follows this structure:

```
### Requirement: [Name]
[Description of the behavior]

#### Scenario: [Scenario Name]
- GIVEN [initial state]
- WHEN [action taken]
- THEN [expected outcome]
- AND [additional outcomes]
```

The guide states: "Requirements are the 'what' — they state what the system should do" and "Scenarios are the 'when' — they provide concrete examples that can be verified."

## Proposal Format

A proposal (`proposal.md`) contains:

- **Intent** — why the change is needed
- **Scope** — what's in and out of scope
- **Approach** — high-level technical strategy

## Tasks Format

Tasks (`tasks.md`) use a checklist with hierarchical numbering:

```
## [Section heading]
- [ ] [Number] [Task description]
- [ ] [Number.Number] [Subtask]
```

## Key Rules

"Scenarios are testable (you could write an automated test for them)" and should "cover both happy path and edge cases" using structured Given/When/Then format. Each requirement must include scenarios to make it verifiable.

toolUseResult.result:
# OpenSpec Delta Spec Format

## Delta Section Headers

The three required section headers in delta specs are:

- `## ADDED Requirements`
- `## MODIFIED Requirements`
- `## REMOVED Requirements`

## Requirement and Scenario Format

Each requirement follows this structure:

```
### Requirement: [Name]
[Description of the behavior]

#### Scenario: [Scenario Name]
- GIVEN [initial state]
- WHEN [action taken]
- THEN [expected outcome]
- AND [additional outcomes]
```

The guide states: "Requirements are the 'what' — they state what the system should do" and "Scenarios are the 'when' — they provide concrete examples that can be verified."

## Proposal Format

A proposal (`proposal.md`) contains:

- **Intent** — why the change is needed
- **Scope** — what's in and out of scope
- **Approach** — high-level technical strategy

## Tasks Format

Tasks (`tasks.md`) use a checklist with hierarchical numbering:

```
## [Section heading]
- [ ] [Number] [Task description]
- [ ] [Number.Number] [Subtask]
```

## Key Rules

"Scenarios are testable (you could write an automated test for them)" and should "cover both happy path and edge cases" using structured Given/When/Then format. Each requirement must include scenarios to make it verifiable.

Usage: openspec [options] [command]

AI-native system for spec-driven development

Commands:

attachment.content:
- content-research-writer: Assists in writing high-quality content by conducting research, adding citations, improving hooks, iterating on outlines, and providing real-time feedback on each section. Transforms your writing process from solo effort to collaborative partnership.
- hyperframes: Create video compositions, animations, title cards, overlays, captions, voiceovers, audio-reactive visuals, and scene transitions in HyperFrames HTML. Use when asked to build any HTML-based video content, add captions or subtitles synced to audio, generate text-to-speech narration, create audio-reactive animation (beat sync, glow, pulse driven by music), add animated text highlighting (marker sweeps, hand-drawn circles, burst lines, scribble, sketchout), or add transitions between scenes (crossfades, wipes, reveals, shader transitions). Covers composition authoring, timing, media, and the full video production workflow. For CLI commands (init, lint, preview, render, transcribe, tts) see the hyperframes-cli skill.
- website-to-hyperframes: Capture a website and create a HyperFrames video from it. Use when: (1) a user provides a URL and wants a video, (2) someone says "capture this site", "turn this into a video", "make a promo from my site", (3) the user wants a social ad, product tour, or any video based on an existing website, (4) the user shares a link and asks for any kind of video content. Even if the user just pastes a URL — this is the skill to use.
- commit: Create commit messages following Sentry conventions. Use when committing code changes, writing commit messages, or formatting git history. Follows conventional commits with Sentry-specific issue references.
- remotion-to-hyperframes: Translate an existing Remotion (React-based) video composition into a HyperFrames HTML composition. Use ONLY when the user explicitly asks to port, convert, migrate, translate, or rewrite a Remotion composition as HyperFrames — for example "port my Remotion project to HyperFrames", "convert this Remotion code to HyperFrames", "migrate from Remotion", "translate this Remotion comp", or "rewrite this as HyperFrames HTML". Do NOT use when (a) the user is authoring a NEW HyperFrames composition, even if they have or are A/B-testing a similar Remotion video; (b) the user mentions Remotion in passing without asking for migration; (c) the user shares Remotion code as reference material rather than asking for a translation; (d) the user asks for "the same video as my Remotion one" without explicitly asking to migrate the source — treat that as a fresh HyperFrames build. When in doubt, default to authoring a native HyperFrames composition with the `hyperframes` skill instead. Skill detects unsupported patterns (useState, useEffect with side effects, async calculateMetadata, third-party React component libraries, `@remotion/lambda` features) and recommends the runtime interop escape hatch instead of attempting a lossy translation.
- hyperframes-cli: HyperFrames CLI tool — hyperframes init, lint, inspect, preview, render, transcribe, tts, doctor, browser, info, upgrade, compositions, docs, benchmark. Use when scaffolding a project, linting, validating, inspecting visual layout in compositions, previewing in the studio, rendering to video, transcribing audio, generating TTS, or troubleshooting the HyperFrames environment.
- using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
- gaia-video-animations: Create high-quality Remotion video animations for GAIA product commercials. Use when building, modifying, or creating new scenes for the GAIA promotional video (apps/video), creating Apple-style motion design commercials in Remotion, or when working on any video animation in the GAIA monorepo. Covers scene architecture, animation patterns, typography, transitions, sound design, and aesthetic rules derived from 72 production conversations. Also use when the user asks to create a new video, add scenes, fix animation timing, or improve visual quality of Remotion compositions.
- langsmith-fetch: Debug LangChain and LangGraph agents by fetching execution traces from LangSmith Studio. Use when debugging agent behavior, investigating errors, analyzing tool calls, checking memory operations, or examining agent performance. Automatically fetches recent traces and analyzes execution patterns. Requires langsmith-fetch CLI installed.
- motion-design-videos: Produce an Apple-keynote-quality motion-design video in HyperFrames (HTML + GSAP compositions) — kinetic typography plus real product UI, scored to a real music track, rendered at 4K. Use when asked to make a motion-design video, an Apple-style launch/product film, a feature-announcement video, a hype/promo reel or sizzle, ESPECIALLY in HyperFrames, or when porting motion-studio/Remotion components into a HyperFrames video. The engine is HyperFrames — `npx hyperframes` lint/inspect/render, one paused GSAP timeline on window.__timelines, sub-compositions, a reusable component library, and a 4K scale-stage. Covers the full pipeline — discovery, a binding creative brief, a music beat grid, an agent team (scriptwriter, reviewer, component porter, video builder, UX and craft auditors), faithful component porting, the Apple aesthetic and exact palette, sourcing a real royalty-free music track beat-matched to the edit, 4K export, frame-by-frame verification, and the hard-won pitfalls that cost real iterations.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- template-skill: Replace with description of the skill and when Claude should use it.
- waapi: Web Animations API adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when authoring element.animate() motion, Animation currentTime seeking, document.getAnimations(), KeyframeEffect timing, fill modes, or native browser animations that must render deterministically in HyperFrames.
- systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- terminal-title: Automatically updates terminal window title to reflect the current high-level task. Use at the start of every Claude Code session when the user provides their first prompt, and whenever the user switches to a distinctly new high-level task. Helps developers manage multiple Claude Code terminals by providing clear, at-a-glance identification of what each terminal is working on.
- animejs: Anime.js adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when writing Anime.js animations or timelines inside HyperFrames compositions, registering animations on window.__hfAnime, making Anime.js seek-driven and deterministic, or translating Anime.js examples into render-safe HyperFrames HTML.
- hyperframes-registry: Install and wire registry blocks and components into HyperFrames compositions. Use when running hyperframes add, installing a block or component, wiring an installed item into index.html, or working with hyperframes.json. Covers the add command, install locations, block sub-composition wiring, component snippet merging, and registry discovery.
- gaia-ui: Design language and visual conventions for the GAIA web app (apps/web). Use when building new components, pages, tool call sections, chat bubbles, or any UI element in GAIA. Covers the design philosophy, color system, typography, spacing, icons, animations, and all patterns needed to make anything look native to GAIA.
- doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- audit-website: Audit websites for SEO, performance, security, technical, content, and 15 other issue cateories with 230+ rules using the squirrelscan CLI. Returns LLM-optimized reports with health scores, broken links, meta tag analysis, and actionable recommendations. Use to discover and asses website or webapp issues and health.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- domain-name-brainstormer: Generates creative domain name ideas for your project and checks availability across multiple TLDs (.com, .io, .dev, .ai, etc.). Saves hours of brainstorming and manual checking.
- using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- context-engineering-collection: A comprehensive collection of Agent Skills for context engineering, multi-agent architectures, and production agent systems. Use when building, optimizing, or debugging agent systems that require effective context management.
- code-review: Perform code reviews following Sentry engineering practices. Use when reviewing pull requests, examining code changes, or providing feedback on code quality. Covers security, performance, testing, and design review.
- log-convo: Document a great AI coding session as a comprehensive agent-convos MDX entry. Run this after any technically impressive conversation — multi-agent orchestration, production debugging, browser automation, autonomous execution, or any session where something genuinely novel happened. Creates a full blog-post-style entry in src/content/agent-convos/.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- three: Three.js and WebGL adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when creating deterministic Three.js scenes, WebGL canvas layers, AnimationMixer timelines, camera motion, shader-driven visuals, or canvas renders that respond to HyperFrames hf-seek events.
- tailwind: Tailwind CSS v4.2 browser-runtime patterns for HyperFrames compositions. Use when scaffolding or editing projects created with `hyperframes init --tailwind`, writing Tailwind utility classes in composition HTML, adding CSS-first Tailwind v4 theme tokens, debugging v3 vs v4 syntax, or deciding when to compile Tailwind to CSS instead of using the browser runtime.
- portfolio-design-system: Frontend design system and style guide for Aryan Randeriya's portfolio (aryanranderiya.com). Use when building any UI, component, page, animation, or layout in the portfolio project at /Users/aryan/Projects/Web/portfolio. Enforces taste, consistency, and the typographic precision observed in paco.me, emilkowal.ski, endless.design, jakub.kr, and agentation.com.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- agents-md: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create AGENTS.md", "update AGENTS.md", "maintain agent docs", "set up CLAUDE.md", or needs to keep agent instructions concise. Guides discovery of local skills and enforces minimal documentation style.
- find-bugs: Find bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code quality issues in local branch changes. Use when asked to review changes, find bugs, security review, or audit code on the current branch.
- brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- file-organizer: Intelligently organizes your files and folders across your computer by understanding context, finding duplicates, suggesting better structures, and automating cleanup tasks. Reduces cognitive load and keeps your digital workspace tidy without manual effort.

Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.

- seo-audit: When the user wants to audit, review, or diagnose SEO issues on their site. Also use when the user mentions "SEO audit," "technical SEO," "why am I not ranking," "SEO issues," "on-page SEO," "meta tags review," or "SEO health check." For building pages at scale to target keywords, see programmatic-seo. For adding structured data, see schema-markup.
- biome-gritql-plugins: Use when creating custom lint rules in Biome via GritQL plugins, writing .grit files, or debugging why plugin diagnostics are not firing on violations.
- copywriting: When the user wants to write, rewrite, or improve marketing copy for any page — including homepage, landing pages, pricing pages, feature pages, about pages, or product pages. Also use when the user says "write copy for," "improve this copy," "rewrite this page," "marketing copy," "headline help," or "CTA copy." For email copy, see email-sequence. For popup copy, see popup-cro.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- openspec-apply-change: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change. Use when the user wants to start implementing, continue implementation, or work through tasks.
- debug-issue: Systematically debug issues using graph-powered code navigation
- refactor-safely: Plan and execute safe refactoring using dependency analysis
- explore-codebase: Navigate and understand codebase structure using the knowledge graph
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- audit-website: Audit websites for SEO, performance, security, technical, content, and 15 other issue cateories with 230+ rules using the squirrelscan CLI. Returns LLM-optimized reports with health scores, broken links, meta tag analysis, and actionable recommendations. Use to discover and asses website or webapp issues and health.
- mintlify: Build and maintain documentation sites with Mintlify. Use when creating docs pages, configuring navigation, adding components, or setting up API references.
- openspec-explore
- clarify
- layout
- accurate-testing: Write test cases that catch real bugs in production code instead of producing false confidence. Use when writing, reviewing, or planning tests for any codebase. Triggers on: "write tests", "add test coverage", "test this function", "create unit tests", "integration tests", "fix flaky tests", "improve test coverage", "review test quality", "are these tests good", "test plan", or any task involving pytest, vitest, jest, or other test frameworks. Prevents common AI testing pitfalls: over-mocking, testing frameworks instead of production code, fake implementations that bypass real logic, and assertion-free tests.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- distill
- pytest-coverage: Run pytest tests with coverage, discover lines missing coverage, and increase coverage to 100%.
- motion: Build React animations with Motion (Framer Motion) - gestures (drag, hover, tap), scroll effects, spring physics, layout animations, SVG. Bundle: 2.3 KB (mini) to 34 KB (full).

Use when: drag-and-drop, scroll animations, modals, carousels, parallax. Troubleshoot: AnimatePresence exit, list performance, Tailwind conflicts, Next.js "use client".
- delight
- agentation-self-driving
- make-interfaces-feel-better
- seo-geo
- backlink-analyzer
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize new CLAUDE.md file(s) and optional skills/hooks with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

input.new_string:
schema: spec-driven

context: |
  GAIA is a proactive personal AI assistant in an Nx monorepo.
  - apps/web: Next.js 16 (App Router, React 19, TypeScript, Zustand, Tailwind, Biome, HeroUI).
  - apps/desktop: Electron 39 thin shell that loads the apps/web standalone build.
    Main process in src/main (windows/, ipc.ts, server.ts, deep-link.ts, protocol.ts).
    Preload exposes window.api via contextBridge. Splash window already uses
    frame:false + transparent + vibrancy.
  - apps/api: FastAPI + LangGraph two-agent system (comms -> executor), ChromaDB
    semantic tool retrieval, SSE streaming over a Redis channel (stream_manager),
    agent-token auth (middleware/agent_auth.py) for headless clients.
  - apps/voice-agent: LiveKit Agents worker (Deepgram STT, ElevenLabs TTS, Silero VAD)
    whose CustomLLM streams from POST /api/v1/chat-stream. Frontend gets creds from GET /token.
  - libs/wake-word: isomorphic openWakeWord ONNX pipeline ("Hey GAIA" classifier + Silero
    VAD). Web controller uses AudioContext/AudioWorklet/getUserMedia; runs in an Electron
    renderer unchanged. useHeyGaia() hook + WakeWordIndicator exist but are not always-on.
  Conventions: pnpm, Biome (no ESLint/Prettier), Ruff + mypy for Python, full type
  annotations, no inline imports, feature-based organization, design-system card contract,
  icons from @icons (never raw SVG/unicode), HeroUI before custom primitives.
  Base branch is develop (not master).

+    whose CustomLLM streams from POST /api/v1/chat-stream. Frontend gets creds from GET /token.
+  - libs/wake-word: isomorphic openWakeWord ONNX pipeline ("Hey GAIA" classifier + Silero
+    VAD). Web controller uses AudioContext/AudioWorklet/getUserMedia; runs in an Electron
+    renderer unchanged. useHeyGaia() hook + WakeWordIndicator exist but are not always-on.
+  Conventions: pnpm, Biome (no ESLint/Prettier), Ruff + mypy for Python, full type
+  annotations, no inline imports, feature-based organization, design-system card contract,
+  icons from @icons (never raw SVG/unicode), HeroUI before custom primitives.
+  Base branch is develop (not master).
